source.title: Accelerated directed evolution of dye-decolorizing peroxidase using a bacterial extracellular protein secretion system (BENNY)

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The research aimed to enhance the H2O2 tolerance of dye-decolorizing peroxidase 4 (DyP4) from Pleurotus ostreatus using directed evolution. The initial rational design approach (methionine substitution) failed to improve tolerance, prompting a shift to random mutagenesis. A key bottleneck was the laborious screening process, which was addressed by developing a streamlined high-throughput screening (HTS) workflow using an OsmY-based bacterial extracellular protein secretion system (BENNY). The BENNY system enabled extracellular secretion of DyP4, bypassing cell lysis and simplifying the assay. Directed evolution via error-prone PCR (epPCR) and saturation mutagenesis yielded variant 4D4 with improved protein yield, specific activity (2.7-fold increase in kcat/Km), and H2O2 tolerance (7-fold increase in IC50). However, a secretion bottleneck was observed: protein production rate exceeded secretion rate, and partial proteolytic cleavage of the OsmY signal peptide led to intracellular retention. The study demonstrated the first application of OsmY for directed evolution of a haem protein and highlighted limitations in secretion efficiency.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Summarizes the development of a streamlined directed evolution workflow for DyP4 using OsmY-based BENNY. After 3 rounds of epPCR and 1 round of saturation mutagenesis, variant 4D4 (I56V, K109R, N227S, N312S) showed higher protein yield, secretion, specific activity (2.7-fold kcat/Km improvement), and H2O2 tolerance (7-fold IC50 improvement). First report of OsmY for directed evolution and extracellular secretion of a haem protein.
process_signals: Streamlined directed evolution workflow using OsmY secretion
Multiple rounds of mutagenesis: 3 epPCR + 1 saturation
Key variant 4D4 with multiple improved phenotypes
methods: OsmY-based bacterial extracellular protein secretion system (BENNY)

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: DyPs are haem peroxidases with unique properties (low pH activity, wide substrate range) and potential in lignocellulosic degradation. DyP4 from P. ostreatus is a fungal DyP with high stability but is inhibited by H2O2. The goal is to enhance H2O2 tolerance via directed evolution. To accelerate evolution, an OsmY-based secretion system (BENNY) is proposed to simplify screening by enabling extracellular secretion in E. coli, bypassing cell lysis. First application of OsmY for directed evolution and haem protein secretion.
process_signals: Problem: DyP4 inhibited by H2O2
Hypothesis: OsmY fusion can enable extracellular secretion for simplified screening
Goal: Accelerate directed evolution workflow

section_gists.heading: Materials and methods
section_gists.gist: Section header only; no content provided.

section_gists.heading: Materials
section_gists.gist: Lists suppliers for chemicals, enzymes, kits, and oligonucleotides. No process information.

section_gists.heading: Results and discussion
section_gists.gist: Section header only; no content provided.

section_gists.heading: Methionine substitution did not improve H2O2 tolerance of DyP4
section_gists.gist: Initial rational design: substituted methionine residues (M43L, M77L, M253L, M253F) based on hypothesis that methionine oxidation causes inactivation. All variants were properly folded and active but showed no improvement in H2O2 tolerance. This failure prompted a switch to directed evolution.
process_signals: Hypothesis: Methionine oxidation leads to inactivation; substitution may improve tolerance
Failure: No improvement in H2O2 tolerance for any methionine variant
Decision: Switch to directed evolution
methods: Protein purification and characterization (absorption spectra, activity)
metrics: Optimal H2O2 concentration for WT: 0.25 mM
failures_or_limits: Methionine substitution did not improve H2O2 tolerance

section_gists.heading: N-terminal OsmY fusion resulted in extracellular DyP4 secretion
section_gists.gist: To streamline directed evolution, OsmY was fused to N-terminus of DyP4. Tested secretion in E. coli BL21(DE3) and C41(DE3) at 30°C and 37°C in auto-induction medium (AIM). BL21(DE3) at 30°C gave highest extracellular activity. OsmY fusion bypassed IPTG induction and cell disruption, enabling a simpler HTS. OsmY also worked for other enzymes (β-glucosidase, lipase).
process_signals: Innovation: OsmY fusion for extracellular secretion to simplify screening
Validation: Confirmed secretion via ABTS assay; optimized host and temperature
Advantage: Bypasses induction and cell lysis steps
methods: Expression in E. coli BL21(DE3) and C41(DE3) at 30°C and 37°C in AIM

section_gists.heading: Accelerated HTS with BENNY
section_gists.gist: Adapted BENNY to 96-well format. ABTS assay showed apparent CV of 8% (true CV 22% after background subtraction). Edge effects due to evaporation increased variation; excluding border wells reduced true CV to 11%. For directed evolution, H2O2 concentration was increased to 4 mM (16× optimal) to select for tolerance, where WT retained ~30% activity.
process_signals: HTS validation: CV acceptable for screening
Screening condition: 4 mM H2O2 to select for tolerance
Edge effect identified as source of variation
methods: Background subtraction using empty vector control
metrics: WT relative activity at 4 mM H2O2: ~30%
failures_or_limits: Edge effects from evaporation increase variation

section_gists.heading: Random mutagenesis by epPCR
section_gists.gist: Created random mutagenesis libraries targeting only DyP4 sequence (OsmY unaltered) using 3 epPCR conditions: H (high error: increased Mg2+ + Mn2+), M (medium: increased Mg2+ + imbalanced dNTPs), L (low: 0.01 mM Mn2+). Condition H gave highest error rate but most inactive clones (77.42%), while M and L had ~41% inactive. Libraries used for screening.
process_signals: Strategy: Random mutagenesis only on DyP4, not OsmY
Three error rate conditions to balance diversity and viability
High error rate led to many inactive clones
methods: epPCR with varying Mg2+, Mn2+, and dNTP concentrations
Library quality assessed by PCR product yield and activity screening

section_gists.heading: Improved OsmY-DyP4 variants isolated from all epPCR conditions (H, M and L)
section_gists.gist: Performed 3 rounds of epPCR (screening 558 clones in rounds 1-2, 1116 in round 3) and 1 round of saturation mutagenesis on identified positions. Best variants from each round used as parents. Improved variants found in all conditions. Final variant 4D4 has 4 missense mutations (I56V, K109R, N227S, N312S) and 4 silent mutations. Mutations are not near haem; K109R and N312S increase H-bonds. Average 1.82 nucleotide substitutions per gene, predominantly AT→GC transitions.
process_signals: Iterative evolution: 3 epPCR rounds + saturation mutagenesis
Parental selection: best variant from each round used for next
Mutations accumulated: 4D4 combines beneficial substitutions
Structural analysis: mutations not near haem, some increase H-bonds
methods: epPCR with H, M, L conditions

section_gists.heading: DyP4 variants showed higher protein yield, specific activity and H2O2 tolerance
section_gists.gist: Removed OsmY from WT, 3F6, and 4D4 for characterization. Variants showed higher protein yield (4D4: 2.70 mg vs WT: 1.16 mg from 200 mL culture). UV-vis spectra confirmed proper haem incorporation. Kinetic assays: 3F6 and 4D4 had slightly higher kcat and lower Km, resulting in 2.7-fold improvement in kcat/Km. H2O2 tolerance: IC50 increased from 0.97 mM (WT) to 4.67 mM (3F6) and 7.03 mM (4D4), a 7-fold improvement for 4D4. Optimal H2O2 concentration shifted higher.
process_signals: Validation: OsmY removal to confirm mutations' effects independent of secretion
Multiple improved phenotypes: yield, activity, tolerance
Unexpected Km improvement despite high ABTS in screen
methods: H2O2 tolerance assay with dose-response curve fitting
metrics: Protein yield: WT 1.16 mg, 3F6 1.20 mg, 4D4 2.70 mg
IC50 H2O2: WT 0.97 mM, 3F6 4.67 mM, 4D4 7.03 mM (7-fold improvement)

section_gists.heading: Protein production rate is likely faster than protein secretion rate
section_gists.gist: Observed that OsmY-fused cells had lighter color but still some intracellular protein. SEC of intracellular OsmY-DyP4 showed two peaks: full-length and truncated. Proteomics revealed N-terminal truncation within first 50 amino acids of OsmY, likely cleaving signal peptide (positions 1-28), preventing secretion. Conclusion: production rate > secretion rate, and signal peptide susceptibility leads to intracellular retention.
process_signals: Bottleneck identified: secretion rate limits extracellular yield
Proteolytic cleavage of OsmY signal peptide causes intracellular accumulation
Implication: need to engineer secretion efficiency
methods: Comparison of cell pellet color with/without OsmY
failures_or_limits: Protein production rate exceeds secretion rate
N-terminal OsmY signal peptide susceptible to proteolytic cleavage, leading to non-secreted truncated protein

section_gists.heading: Conclusion
section_gists.gist: Successfully applied OsmY-based BENNY to streamline directed evolution of DyP4, yielding variant 4D4 with improved yield, activity, and H2O2 tolerance. Ongoing work: engineering 4D4 for oxidation of S-type phenolic lignin units. Expanding BENNY toolbox: identifying new secretory proteins, engineering OsmY for higher secretion, extending to other bacterial hosts. Acknowledges limitations and future directions.
process_signals: Achievement: Streamlined workflow and improved variant
Future work: Further engineering for lignin oxidation, expanding BENNY
Limitation: Secretion bottleneck not fully resolved

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research evolved from a rational design failure (methionine substitution) to a successful directed evolution campaign enabled by a novel secretion-based screening system (BENNY).
The BENNY system was critical for high-throughput screening by eliminating cell lysis, but it introduced a secretion bottleneck that limited extracellular yield.

The improved variant 4D4 acquired multiple beneficial mutations through iterative epPCR and saturation mutagenesis, leading to enhanced catalytic efficiency and H2O2 tolerance, though the mutations were not near the active site.
The study demonstrates a generalizable workflow for enzyme engineering, but further optimization of secretion efficiency is needed for industrial application.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What is the mechanistic basis for improved H2O2 tolerance and activity given that mutations are not near the haem?
Can the secretion bottleneck be overcome by engineering OsmY or using alternative secretion systems?
How does 4D4 perform on lignin-related substrates under industrial conditions?
What are the exact screening criteria and hit selection details (likely in supplementary material)?

events.summary: OsmY-based BENNY system designed to streamline directed evolution by enabling extracellular secretion.
Hypothesis that OsmY fusion will enable extracellular secretion of DyP4, simplifying screening.
Three epPCR conditions (H, M, L) designed to generate random mutagenesis libraries with varying error rates.
First report of OsmY for directed evolution and haem protein secretion, but secretion efficiency may be a bottleneck.

events.summary: Determined kinetic constants of DyP4 for ABTS oxidation using Michaelis-Menten model.
Measured H2O2 tolerance of DyP4 and variants by ABTS oxidation with varying H2O2 concentrations.
Fused OsmY to N-terminus of DyP4 to enable extracellular secretion and streamline directed evolution workflow.
Validated BENNY-assisted ABTS assay in 96-well format; obtained apparent CV 8%, true CV 22% (11% excluding border wells).
Created random mutagenesis libraries using three epPCR conditions (H, M, L) targeting only DyP4 sequence; condition H gave highest error rate but most inactive clones.
Sequenced improved variants; average 1.82 nucleotide substitutions per gene, predominantly AT→GC transitions; mutations not near haem.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers methods for kinetic and tolerance assays, failure of rational design, design of secretion system, validation of HTS, screening conditions, mutagenesis library creation, and sequencing analysis.

evidence_bank.quote: To accelerate DyP4 evolution, we used a bacterial extracellular protein secretion system (BENNY) based on the Escherichia coli osmotically-inducible protein Y (OsmY).
this is the first report of applying OsmY to streamline directed evolution workflow and to direct extracellular secretion of a haem protein such as DyP4.
Three error-prone polymerase chain reaction (epPCR) conditions were used in this study: high (H), medium (M) and low (L) error rates.
To our best knowledge, this is the first report of applying OsmY to simplify the directed evolution workflow and to direct the extracellular secretion of a haem protein such as DyP4.
The kinetic constants of DyP4 were determined for ABTS oxidation.
The hydrogen peroxide tolerance for DyP4 was determined using ABTS oxidation.
To streamline the DyP4 directed evolution workflow, we fused E. coli OsmY to the N-terminus of DyP4 and investigated protein secretion
By using OsmY as secretory carrier and fusion protein expression in AIM, we bypassed both (i) protein induction (i.e. addition of IPTG inducer) and (ii) cell disruption
Based on ABTS assay, we obtained an apparent coefficient of variance (CV) of 8% for OsmY-DyP4
If all bordering wells were excluded, the apparent and true CVs were reduced to 4% and 11%, respectively.
we created random mutagenesis libraries of OsmY-DyP4 using 3 epPCR conditions (H, M and L).
mutations were only targeted to the sequence encoding for DyP4, leaving the OsmY sequence unaltered.
On average, there were 1.82 nucleotide substitutions per DyP4 gene
None of these residues is in the vicinity of haem.

K109R and N312S substitutions resulted in higher number of H-bonds formed (3 H-bonds in R109 vs. 1 H-bond in K109 and 4 H-bonds in S312 vs. 3 H-bonds in N312).
To determine the effects of mutations found, we removed the N-terminal OsmY of WT, 3F6 and 4D4, expressed and purified these 3 proteins, and performed further characterizations.
From 200-mL shake flask cultures, the estimated amounts of purified protein obtained were 1.16 mg for WT, 1.20 mg for 3F6 and 2.70 mg for 4D4.
The combination of higher $k_{cat}$ and lower $K_{m}$ resulted in a 2.7-fold improvement in specific activity for both 3F6 and 4D4.

the $H_{2}O_{2}$ tolerance of 4D4 was improved 7×, comparing its $IC_{50}$ value to that of WT.
protein production rate of OsmY-DyP4 is likely faster than its extracellular secretion rate
the signal peptide of OsmY (positions 1–28) was proteolytically cleaved, resulting in no secretion of this truncated protein pool.

Structural characterization of DyPs revealed the presence of a two-domain, $\alpha+\beta$ ferredoxin-like fold that is distinct from the all $\alpha$ -helical fold of the other peroxidase superfamilies (Singh and Eltis 2015).

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Enhance H2O2 tolerance of dye-decolorizing peroxidase 4 (DyP4) from Pleurotus ostreatus using directed evolution, accelerated by a bacterial extracellular protein secretion system (BENNY).

canonical_events.summary: Hypothesis that methionine oxidation causes inactivation; substitution may improve H2O2 tolerance.
Methionine substitution (M43L, M77L, M253L, M253F) did not improve H2O2 tolerance; all variants properly folded and active but no improvement.
Switch to directed evolution; design OsmY-based BENNY system to enable extracellular secretion and streamline high-throughput screening.
Validated BENNY-assisted ABTS assay in 96-well format; apparent CV 8%, true CV 22% (11% excluding border wells); edge effects identified.
Designed three epPCR conditions (H, M, L) with varying error rates to generate random mutagenesis libraries targeting only DyP4 sequence.

Performed 3 rounds of epPCR screening (558 clones in rounds 1-2, 1116 in round 3) with H2O2 concentration increased to 4 mM (16× optimal) to select for tolerance; best variants used as parents for next round.
key_numbers: WT relative activity ~30% at 4 mM H2O2

canonical_events.summary: Performed 1 round of saturation mutagenesis on positions 56, 109, 227, 306, 312, 374 using best epPCR variant 3F6 as template.

Identified final variant 4D4 with 4 missense mutations (I56V, K109R, N227S, N312S) and 4 silent mutations; average 1.82 nucleotide substitutions per gene, predominantly AT→GC transitions; mutations not near haem.
Removed OsmY from WT, 3F6, and 4D4 to characterize mutations independently; variants showed higher protein yield (4D4: 2.70 mg vs WT: 1.16 mg from 200 mL culture).
Kinetic analysis showed 2.7-fold improvement in kcat/Km for 3F6 and 4D4 (WT: 4.31, 3F6: 11.78, 4D4: 11.60 mM-1 s-1).
H2O2 tolerance assay showed 7-fold improvement in IC50 for 4D4 (WT: 0.97 mM, 4D4: 7.03 mM).
Protein production rate exceeds secretion rate; proteolytic cleavage of OsmY signal peptide leads to intracellular retention of truncated protein.
Ongoing work to further engineer 4D4 for oxidation of S-type phenolic lignin units and expand BENNY toolbox.

After demonstrating improved variant, secretion bottleneck identified as limitation.

must_cover_points: Initial rational design failure with methionine substitution.
Design and validation of BENNY system for extracellular secretion and HTS.
Directed evolution workflow: epPCR library design, screening conditions (4 mM H2O2), iterative rounds.
Identification of 4D4 variant with 4 missense mutations.
Characterization of 4D4: improved yield, 2.7-fold kcat/Km, 7-fold IC50.
Secretion bottleneck: production rate > secretion rate, signal peptide cleavage.
